topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy paper felt impossibly cold beneath my fingertips. I stared at the meticulously printed paragraphs, each sentence a carefully constructed narrative of my life—my childhood dreams, my career milestones, even the seemingly intimate details of my private struggles. A tremor started low in my stomach, a tight, unpleasant clenching that radiated outward. I reread the passage about my first heartbreak, the description so vivid, so *accurate*, that a profound sense of disorientation washed over me. My hands began to tremble, and I had to grip the edge of the desk to keep from dropping the document. Who had done this? The air in the room suddenly felt thin, suffocating. I pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, my breath catching in shallow, uneven gasps.

[story 2]

It wasn't until I saw the dates, the names of people I’d never met in that context, that the reality began to fracture. The document lay open on the coffee table, mocking me with its precision. A strange hollowness expanded in my chest, a sudden vacuum where certainty used to reside. I picked up a pen, intending to cross out a line, but my hand shook too violently. The ink blurred as I tried to make a mark, a futile attempt to assert control over the chaos unfolding inside me. I sat back down, my posture rigid, my knuckles white where I gripped the pen. My gaze swept over the text again, slower this time, absorbing the implications. A dull ache settled behind my eyes, and I could feel a tightness constricting my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

The revelation hit me like a physical blow. I stood frozen in the doorway of the study, the sheaf of papers trembling in my grasp. Every word confirmed a version of myself that was alien, a stranger wearing my skin. My vision tunneled slightly; the edges of the room seemed to soften and blur into an indistinct haze. I let the papers slip from my fingers, and they fluttered silently to the thick rug. I took a deliberate step backward, my heel catching on the edge of the rug, forcing me to steady myself. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips—a ragged intake of air—as I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to force the distorted images to recede. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming of my pulse against my ribs.
